author: @HunterNX_s
text: Really when it comes to making objects in curved shapes or with interior, the laser is not the best candidate for the job, but still has the option of using milling with a router. I don't know if it's faster than a 3D Printer, but the main advantage is working with denser and harder materials like metals.

author: @ThunderDivine

author: @corresandberg
text: As Elon is saying and in essence what Rob might mean is this: Making a mock-up VTOL or Space Rocket is easy (for some, it seems anyway) but make machines that build 1000's and per week or per minute is something else. 3D and Laser cutting at the moment seems to me to be more efficient for mock-ups. For large scale casting, plastic or metal, seems more efficient but how would I know...really. Experiment with it. What ever you do. Don't get stuck, in your way of thinking. I personally are in-stuck with metal, I need metal to weld, but hard noch impossible to get hands on if you are not a company where I am at. So I am thinking of using old bycycles instead of plain steel.

author: @3amali1
text: Nice work, Rob. Promising approach but as you pointed out it has its limitations. Last year I had a student investigating laser cutting and welding of polymer sheets as a 3D printing technique. The process scientifically is known as "sheet lamination additive manufacturing" but usually uses separate bonding process. In this case the student was able to identify the cutting and welding parameters of 100 micron polyester sheets and actually managed to make a simple face shield in the summer in few minutes in a single process!  A short video was made on the process, search on Youtube for (FMPRG) it's one of the few videos of the groups channel.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate I will do

author: @alanesq1
text:
I have a CNC router which similar to the laser cutter is very quick and reliable to produce parts.  
One of the advantages I think is that it is surprisingly easy to build your own  (see GRBL which runs on an Arduino).  You can fit a pen and you have a plotter or install a vinyl cutting head (£10 on eBay) and cut labels or even fit a small laser and you have a laser engraver.
I can quickly draw the basic 2d shape I require in Inkscape, feed this in to Laserweb to produce the Gcode and then cut it out on my CNC router all within a few minutes.    (btw - very good for cutting wooden gears)

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering indeed will be a different world if I can finish my replicator level system that can be packed in a boot of a car, breaks down anything you put in to use as printing consumables along with the sorting process of all the various nano particles that are formed like copper oxide from any copper or copper bearing component broken down, ammonia, water, silicon dioxide particles ect, LOL

and it can replicate itself thus feed in junk not wanted, out pops parts that you can give to a friend who can slap them together and have his own replicator level printer, thus it spreads a bit like a virus, you know someone who has one, you end up with your house infected with a printer that can print damn near anything, and anything it is too slow you can print a work around like zeolite for converting ammonia water CO2 into amino acids or sugar components so pushing those out allows you to take these more complex molecules to print out food, that is both edible and has nutrient value :)

author: @NorthernKitty
text: That settles it.  My goal for next year is to get a CNC router with a laser cutting attachment.  😋   A lot of the pieces I've done recently have been pretty flat (spacers, lids, brackets, etc) that have just been frustrating to wait 5 hours for.

replies.author: @alanesq1
replies.text:
I built my own a while back and find it really useful as once you have a bit of practice you can design and cut out the part you want in minutes and most times it works first try.
I used GRBL running on an Arduino although the one which runs on an ESP32 looks like it could be really good.   I suggest using Inkscape to design the part then Laserweb to produce the Gcode.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @alanesq1 Thanks for the tips!  I'll give some thought to building one, since I can certainly print some of the parts and have been slowly acquiring others just by the nature of working with 3D printing (spare stepper motors, v-slot rails, etc).  I also have a spare Arduino collecting dust that I've been itching to find a purpose for.  Maybe that's a better approach for me, it gives me something to focus on all winter!  😋

replies.author: @alanesq1
replies.text:
@NorthernKitty The main thing is to make it as sturdy/stiff as you can (especially compared to a 3d printer).  I think using two stepper motors on the main rails (one each side) would be a better approach than the single one I used to reduce flex.  
I was on a very tight budget so I just bought the cheapest components I could find on eBay and have had no problem with any of them (e.g. stepper controllers £6 each) and most of it is built from plywood.

author: @BroversXproductions
text: Great video! I've actually been wanting to get into 3D printing recently. Its worth noting that actually printing parts is only half the picture; post-processing is a whole other thing. Taking a 3D print and annealing it, vapor smoothing, remelting, and even electroplating it. There's also lost PLA casting which is a messy way of getting metal parts from a 3D printer, however some people may not be able to accommodate this. I still have all of this to explore! However, I might start with just a cheap printer as a test-run.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's what I would do mate - when I am interested in something - I start cheap lol

author: @GENcELL2014
text:
Higher end 3d printing materials are pretty good and hold up but most of those actual rugged materials are well above 300C on the printers hotend. So beyond average printers most people have, not to say materials bellow 300C aren't capable but they tend to have dimensional stability issues whenever even a little heat is present.

Because of that I view the low cost 3d printers and lower temperature materials as a stepping stone to an actual end use part. Essentially use 3d prints that are finished to remove layer lines for better surface finishes and make a silicone, fiberglass or carbon fiber mold that end use resin or composite parts can be produced from. You get the geometric freedom and ability to make low cost masters for making molds and the ability to make parts faster with better material properties.

In that respect 3d printing for majority of things is superior to laser cutting even when laminated stack ups are made. That said for making masters you could make laminates with laser cut pieces for flat large features and 3d printing for smaller more complex 3d features.

I personally have a couple 3d printers, resin casting, composites equipment and a capable CNC router that can fairly easily make aluminum, copper, brass, composite and plastic parts(30"x40" milling area).

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text: Another workshop I would love to  spend a week in. I have a little electroplating setup and a few small projects going in mine

replies.author: @GENcELL2014
replies.text:
@karlmyers6518 thanks, cool. I also have a small electroplating tank, it's a square container that holds around 2-2.5 gallons of nickel plating solution. Put in a 12W water pump that I 3d print custom flow channels and features to put nickel strips that  make electrical contact with part. Pump is so parts holes or crevasses don't build hydrogen and oxygen creating a gas pocket and not electroplating properly. I bought a Caswell electroplating kit, it's worked perfectly except for the pump it came with, it was like 3 or 4 watts. Not strong enough and can get freezed up from crystal formation when it gets taken out and plating solution evaporates.

My workspaces is for developing and eventually producing very well engineered lithium battery assembly solutions.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate high end printers are awesome but so are high end laser cutters - you have to compare apples and apples mate - there is a bit of apples and oranges going on here I feel

replies.author: @GENcELL2014
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering more pointing out how cheap 3d printing materials are lacking but can open up part production in ways that can be impressive when you are using the prints to make molds. The fact I can easily make geometry that is fairly accurate that can eventually make resin or composite parts that are stronger than metal is awesome in my opinion. Laser cutting can do the same but not as easily with the same level of detail and complexity.

We're more talking about ways to make good usable parts. From my experience molds are the way to go regardless if you have a CNC laser, router, or 3d printer. For prototyping and figuring out tolerances you don't need to usually make molds but once that process is done I'm on to mold making to get parts with rigidity, stability and isotropic properties that 3d printing usually can't ever reach.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @GENcELL2014 I don't disagree mate

author: @kevin_delaney
text: Depends on the price point. Depends on what you gotta do. Laser cutters can be pricey like Epilog, good old industrial printers are far less expensive, but slower, often very proprietary as well. Well said conclusion though, I completely agree.

replies.author: @earlyriser438
replies.text: Laser cutters can be DIY'd fairly simply using the guts from a junked dvd player, lotta tutorials out there, and the components to supercharge the beam cutter are a mere click away from Chinese online wholesalers.

replies.author: @kevin_delaney
replies.text: @earlyriser438 While that's true, I wouldn't advise most to do it. You gotta have a lot of respect for lasers, the GlowForge is a good inexpensive laser system. It takes an uncommon kind of mindset to make a laser cutter. DIY 3D printers are far less dangerous, but typically far less precise. With my experience, anything under about $2k or $2500 is....in my personal opinion, more trouble than it's worth the majority of the time.

text: Hi, are the layers adhered together prior to laser stacking?  If not is it likely they will delaminates during use?  I ask out of lack of knowledge / experience of how well the layers are ‘welded’ together.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @earlyriser438
text:
I've been toying between these two technologies but being broke I find fulfillment going full old-school. 

Busted out my drawknife and chisels and sent some wood shavings flying. So satisfying, and the smell!

Beats the whine of machines and the smell of melted plastics, for me at least.

Harder to mass produce but I prefer art to industry anyway.

Great work as usual Robert, I'd love to see some more ceramic work from you.

Graphene-reinforced DIY aerospace grade ceramics perhaps?

Cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I tend to mess around with both mate as you know - I love hand work and to me it is often the fastest method - I hate to say this as it sounds big headed - but I think I am pretty skilled in the use of hand tools - a lot of folks aren't and machines help with that

replies.author: @earlyriser438
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering so true. I started teaching the neighbor kid the basics of shaping wood by just dragging a stick on concrete. He was amazed that such a feat was even physically possible and it made my heart glow to see his eyes light up with creative accomplishment.

He wants me to teach him how to carve a lacrosse stick now. 

He was obsessing over mine that I carved back in the 90s but he was crestfallen when I told him a man's lacrosse stick goes with him to the grave, plus mine is autographed by Miles Thompson.

Tiger Woods is to golf what Miles Thompson is to lacrosse, and I went to high school with his mother in law. 😎

author: @TheKlink
text: what's the term for heating up to just below melting point and then cooling down over a long period? so it doesn't shatter? 3d printing is probably great for that stuff.

replies.author: @earlyriser438
replies.text: Tempering?

replies.author: @Detective_Tapp
replies.text: annealing? like in glass where you give the molecules time to rearrange to form a better suited molecular structure. 3d printing is different cause you don't really change the molecular structure, but you adhere layers due to them being hot. I think that laser cutting is better for simple assembly products and that 3d printing is much better for more complex parts that have multiple dimensions.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: annealing
